data.title: Biogen’s Alloy Antisense Deal Adds New Dimension To Genetic Medicine Bet
data.description:
• Biogen and Alloy Therapeutics have entered a new collaboration to develop multi target antisense oligonucleotide drugs using Alloy’s AntiClastic ASO platform.
  • The partnership focuses on several undisclosed genetic disease targets and marks Biogen’s first announced use of an advanced antisense platform across multiple programs.

For investors watching NasdaqGS:BIIB at a share price of $177.11, this move adds a fresh angle to the story. The stock shows mixed medium to long term performance, with a 56.2% return over 1 year contrasted with declines of 38.9% over 3 years and 34.5% over 5 years. In that context, a broader push into genetic medicine is another factor to consider when assessing the company’s R&D profile and risk mix.

This collaboration extends Biogen’s footprint beyond its traditional focus areas into antisense based genetic medicine, using a platform designed to address long standing efficacy and safety challenges in the class. For now, the diseases and timelines are undisclosed. The main takeaway is that Biogen is tying more of its discovery efforts to this technology, which may affect how its pipeline and competitive position develop over time.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Biogen by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Biogen.

2 things going right for Biogen that this headline doesn't cover.

This multi target antisense deal pushes Biogen further into genetic medicine in a way that complements its existing neurology and rare disease focus. By licensing Alloy’s AntiClastic ASO platform rather than building a similar capability in house, Biogen gains access to a technology specifically designed to address potency and safety constraints that have limited some antisense approaches. For you as an investor, the key point is less the undisclosed targets and more the decision to tie multiple programs to a single platform that aims to improve tissue targeting and therapeutic index in RNA level drugs. That brings added scientific and execution risk, but can also create operating leverage if several programs move forward using shared tools and know how.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Biogen Narrative

  • The push into antisense therapies aligns with the existing narrative that Biogen is leaning on a broad neurology and specialty pipeline, including genetic medicines, to add more “shots on goal” in high unmet need conditions.
  • Relying on a partner platform for multiple undisclosed targets adds another layer of dependency on external technology, which could challenge the narrative if clinical progress is slower than expected or if safety or tolerability constraints appear.
  • The use of Alloy’s AI enabled discovery and AntiClastic formats, and any future data from these programs, are not explicitly reflected in the current narrative, so this collaboration may introduce upside or downside that is not yet captured in existing assumptions.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Biogen to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Antisense drugs have a history of potency, biodistribution, and safety challenges, so there is a real possibility that programs using the AntiClastic platform encounter efficacy or tolerability hurdles in the clinic.
  • ⚠️ Biogen has flagged large one off items affecting reported results in the past, and this sort of multi target R&D collaboration can add milestone and research charges that make near term earnings more volatile.
  • 🎁 The partnership fits with Biogen’s focus on genetic and neuroimmune diseases, giving the company a new RNA based toolset that can be applied to several indications without acquiring an entire platform company.
  • 🎁 If the AntiClastic ASO format continues to show higher potency versus traditional gapmer antisense in internal and partner programs, Biogen could build differentiated assets in areas where competitors such as Ionis Pharmaceuticals, Vertex, or Eli Lilly are also investing.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, pay attention to how quickly Biogen and Alloy move from discovery into named development candidates, and whether Biogen begins to disclose specific indications or timelines around these antisense programs. Any early clinical readouts on safety and target engagement will matter more than the upfront deal terms, especially given Biogen’s existing exposure to antisense through SPINRAZA and other collaborations. It is also worth watching how the company frames R&D spend and in process research and development charges linked to this agreement in upcoming quarters, because that will influence how clean the earnings picture looks versus underlying operating performance.

data.title: Medtronic And GE HealthCare Tie Imaging Push To Neurosurgery Growth Potential

  • The system combines live ultrasound with pre-operative MRI and CT data, supported by AI tools, to support neurosurgeons during complex cranial procedures.
  • The platform is scheduled to debut at the 2026 AANS Scientific Meeting, with features including specialized algorithms, color Doppler, remote controls, and single-use accessories.

For investors tracking NYSE:MDT, this move sits within Medtronic's core focus on advanced surgical technologies for high-value procedures. Digital integration between navigation and imaging matters because neurosurgeons face challenges such as brain shift that can limit the precision of pre-operative scans alone. A tighter, AI-supported workflow may make Medtronic's ecosystem more relevant for hospitals evaluating complex cranial surgery solutions.

This launch also reflects a broader push across medtech toward connected, data-rich operating rooms and closer collaboration between device makers and imaging specialists. If hospitals respond positively to this type of integrated platform, it could influence capital spending decisions and partnership models across neurosurgery and other complex surgical fields.

Approach 1: General Motors Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company is worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a present value.

For General Motors, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow stands at about $16.29b, and analysts provide explicit forecasts out to 2027, with Simply Wall St extrapolating further to 2035. For example, projected free cash flow for 2026 is $10.45b and for 2035 it is $12.76b, all in $ terms.

Discounting these cash flows back to today produces an estimated intrinsic value of $120.35 per share. Compared with the current share price, this DCF model indicates the stock is 36.2% undervalued.

For readers, the key takeaway is that under these cash flow assumptions the current price is lower than the modelled value of the underlying business.

Approach 2: General Motors Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like General Motors, the P/E ratio is often a useful quick check because it tells you how many dollars you are paying for each dollar of current earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E usually reflects how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk tends to come with a lower multiple.

General Motors currently trades on a P/E of 21.8x. That sits above the Auto industry average of 17.1x, but below the peer group average of 56.8x, which suggests investors are pricing it more cautiously than some peers but higher than the broader industry.

This gives a more tailored anchor for valuation than a simple comparison with industry averages or peers, which may have very different profiles.

data.title: Should Skeena’s US$750M Note Deal and Stream Cut Reshape Expectations for Skeena Resources (TSX:SKE)?
data.description:
• Skeena Resources has recently priced and completed a US$750 million offering of 8.500% senior secured notes due 2031, using the funds to reduce its existing gold stream, fund interest reserves, advance construction at the Eskay Creek project, and bolster its cash position.
  • A key implication is the 66.67% reduction in Eskay Creek’s gold streaming commitments, which shifts more future project output back to Skeena.
  • We’ll now explore how this large note issuance and stream reduction shape Skeena Resources’ investment narrative and risk-reward profile.

What Is Skeena Resources' Investment Narrative?

For Skeena Resources, the core belief is that Eskay Creek can transition from a high-potential development story into a producing gold‑silver mine on roughly the current schedule, and that today’s valuation reflects that path. The new US$750 million note issue and gold stream reduction fit directly into that narrative: Skeena is swapping undrawn, more flexible funding and a cost over‑run backstop for a large, fixed 8.5% debt load in order to keep more of Eskay Creek’s future production. That meaningfully reshapes near‑term catalysts and risks. On the positive side, construction funding now looks more clearly addressed and the company has hard‑wired its interest costs for several years, which may support confidence around the 2027 start‑up milestones. On the risk side, leverage and interest expense are now front and center, alongside the usual execution and cost risks that come with a partially built, higher‑budget mine.

However, a higher fixed debt burden introduces a new pressure point that shareholders should understand. Skeena Resources' shares have been on the rise but are still potentially undervalued. Find out how large the opportunity might be.

What Extendicare’s New Debt Deal Means for Shareholders

Extendicare (TSX:EXE) recently priced a $450 million offering of 4.345% senior unsecured notes due 2031, with plans to fully repay its term credit facility and other existing debt.

The company expects this refinancing to lower its cost of capital and increase financial flexibility, which it states would position it to pursue additional scale in Canada’s fragmented seniors care sector.

See our latest analysis for Extendicare.

Extendicare’s recent refinancing news comes as the share price sits at CA$29.52, with a 90 day share price return of 34.86% and a very large 3 year total shareholder return of 427.25%, suggesting momentum has been building rather than fading.

If this kind of sustained move has your attention, it could be a good moment to broaden your watchlist and check out 6 healthcare AI stocks.

Price-to-Earnings of 28.8x: Is It Justified?

Extendicare trades on a P/E of 28.8x, which sits slightly below the 31.4x average for its direct peers but above the wider North American Healthcare industry on 22.7x.

The P/E ratio compares the current share price to earnings per share, so a higher multiple often reflects the market paying up for earnings quality or growth. In Extendicare’s case, earnings are described as high quality, with 28.5% earnings growth over the past year and a 5 year earnings growth rate of 33.6% per year.

That context helps explain why investors may accept a higher P/E than the broader Healthcare industry. Earnings growth over the past year exceeded the Healthcare industry’s 15.7%, revenue is forecast to grow 13.7% per year compared to 4.9% for the Canadian market, and Return on Equity sits at a high 25.9%. At the same time, Extendicare is trading above the CA$24.20 fair value estimate from the SWS DCF model. This suggests the market is not purely relying on current cash flow assumptions.

Compared with the broader North American Healthcare industry average P/E of 22.7x, Extendicare’s 28.8x multiple implies investors are paying a premium for its earnings profile. Versus its closer peer set on 31.4x, that same 28.8x looks more restrained. This could indicate the stock is priced roughly in line with what that peer group commands for similar earnings characteristics.

data.title: A Look At BWX Technologies (BWXT) Valuation As It Pursues New Uranium Enrichment License
data.description:
BWX Technologies (BWXT) has notified the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission of plans to seek a uranium enrichment license for a new Erwin, Tennessee facility, which is directly tied to its $1.5b NNSA defense fuels contract.

See our latest analysis for BWX Technologies.

The uranium enrichment plan comes as BWX Technologies’ share price has climbed to US$230.29, with a 30 day share price return of 17.55% and a 1 year total shareholder return of 129.46%. This suggests momentum has been building alongside recent contract and facility developments.

If this nuclear-focused story has your attention, it could be a good moment to see what else is happening across the sector via our 93 nuclear energy infrastructure stocks

With BWX Technologies now trading at US$230.29 and sitting only about 1% below the average analyst price target, the key question is simple: are you looking at a genuine opportunity or a market already pricing in future growth?

Advertisement

Most Popular Narrative: 77.1% Overvalued

According to the most widely followed narrative, BWX Technologies' current share price of $230.29 sits far above an estimated fair value of $130. That gap sets the tone for a sharply skeptical view of how much optimism is currently priced in.

BWX Technologies is, sin duda, una empresa de defensa de alta calidad, pero cotiza con una valoración propia de un software especulativo de alto margen. El precio actual no ofrece ningún margen de seguridad para los riesgos probables de una integración compleja de M&A ni para los inevitables retrasos regulatorios nucleares.

Read the complete narrative.

Curious what kind of growth profile and profit margins could justify a fair value far below $230? The narrative leans heavily on earnings quality, projected cash generation and a future valuation multiple more commonly associated with premium growth names, not traditional industrial contractors. The precise assumptions behind that gap are where the story really gets interesting.

According to Zaleznog, the narrative rests on BWX Technologies being a high quality nuclear and defense supplier that is currently priced more like a high growth software business. The fair value estimate of $130 contrasts with the market price and reflects a view that forecast earnings growth, profit margins and the chosen future P/E multiple should be anchored to what is described as a more traditional industrial profile rather than a high growth technology profile.

On the cash flow side, the narrative highlights BWX Technologies' recent focus on acquisitions and the impact this has on valuation work. It points out that spending on mergers and acquisitions is described as multiple times higher than capital expenditure, which feeds into the way long term cash generation is assessed and discounted. That acquisition focus, combined with expectations for how quickly new assets contribute to profits, is central to the narrative's discounted cash flow style fair value.

BWX Technologies' balance sheet structure and debt profile also feature in the story. The narrative emphasizes the existence of a sizeable term loan refinancing in 2027 and links this to the required discount rate of 7.70%, arguing that funding risk and interest costs need to be reflected when comparing fair value to the current $230.29 share price. This perspective sits alongside the company's current high reported return on equity of 26.75%, which other data suggests is influenced by leverage.

Pulling these strands together, the narrative concludes that BWX Technologies, with revenue of $3.20b and net income of $328.95m, does not offer much of a valuation buffer at today's price. It weighs forecast earnings growth of about 13.22% a year, a future P/E multiple of 35 and a profit margin just above 10% against a discount rate below 8% and arrives at a fair value per share that is materially below the current market level.

Result: Fair Value of $130 (OVERVALUED)
